summary: A dispute about the exact Hebrew formula priests used when announcing dawn. An extended discussion about the time of the afternoon prayer (mincha) includes traditions about the Patriarchs: Abraham observed mincha at the blackening of walls; Rav Yosef and Rava debate whether pre-Sinai conduct can establish law. Abraham is said to have observed the entire Torah, including the eruv tavshilin. The page also addresses the confusion incident: a cloudy day was mistaken for dawn, causing premature slaughter of the tamid.

glossary.en: The afternoon prayer and the afternoon grain-offering; Abraham observed mincha when walls began to darken at midday.
A symbolic food preparation combining dishes to permit cooking on a holiday that falls before Shabbat.
Shield; title of the first blessing of the Amida, which praises God for the merits of the Patriarchs.

rashiTranslations.en: Dispute about the exact formula: 'Is the entire sky illuminated?' or 'Has the morning star risen?'
If the eastern sky is already illuminated, why ask? Implication about the formula.
Rav Safra: Abraham's afternoon prayer time was when walls begin to blacken.
Rav Yosef: can we derive law from Abraham's pre-Sinai conduct?
Rava: Rav Yosef's objection was about deriving from Abraham's practice - not that Abraham didn't do it.
The Gemara asks: what does 'walls beginning to blacken' mean exactly?
Egypt had its own yeshiva: 'Go and gather the elders of Israel' (Exodus 3:16).
Eliezer, Abraham's servant, was an elder in the academy.
Rav: Abraham observed the entire Torah, as the verse says 'because Abraham obeyed My voice' (Gen. 26:5).
Abraham also observed eruv tavshilin - derived from 'Torah and mitzvot' (plural) in the same verse.
Mishna: Matya ben Shmuel announces 'Is the entire eastern sky illuminated?'
Who makes this announcement: someone standing on the ground or on a high place?
Alternative: the person on the ground asks; the person on the elevated spot responds.
The confusion incident: priests slaughtered the tamid before dawn due to moonlight.
Practical question: what difference does it make if a cloudy day resembles a complete darkness?
Rav Nachman: the pale light of sun through clouds is more harmful than sunlight.
And needed to be roasted before Shabbat began.
As stated in that tractate: it is offered at its proper time.
From when the shadows incline, as the Mishna teaches.
The tamid was slaughtered at six and a half hours.
If 'from when the walls whiten' is already considered afternoon.
Slaughter it from when the walls whiten - that is from the start of the seventh hour.
The walls were not perfectly straight and vertical.
The walls were wider at the bottom and narrowed upward.
Therefore the top edge does not cast shadow on the wall face.
Until the sun moves westward.
Astronomy - expertise in the movement of the stars and the beginning of the hours.
As taught in Bava Batra (16b): 'God blessed Abraham with everything.'
Abraham had great astrological expertise.
All the kings of east and west came early to his door to inquire about the constellations.
Drawing and watering - a notarikon for Damascus.
Seven commandments - those given to all the children of Noah.
But the Torah was not given to him.
Even eruv tavshilin - which is not a law from Sinai but a rabbinic enactment.
That the later Sages were destined to enact.
He himself interprets - he asks and answers his own question.
The one on the roof said it; 'until it reaches Hebron' - this is a question.
And he who stands on the ground - said 'yes'; how did the one on the ground say it?
The one who stands on the ground asked 'has the entire eastern sky lit up.'
And the one on the roof answered 'until Hebron' - meaning yes, it has lit that far.
And the ground person said 'yes' - meaning: that is what I am asking, has it lit until Hebron.
We find in the Jerusalem Talmud (ch. 3 h. 1) that they mention Hebron.
To invoke the merit of the patriarchs buried there.
Can they be confused? The column of sunlight looks like the column of moonlight.
The column of the sun - at dawn it splits and rises like a palm tree.
'And pillars of smoke' (Joel 3) - because it rises erect.
A cloudy day - the sky is covered with clouds and the column is not visible through the mist.
Only in the gaps between clouds in many places between cloud and cloud.
Learn from this - since he says 'on a cloudy day it splits in many places.'
A completely overcast day - even in shadowed spots the sun breaks through.
It is not directed through a specific window; it spreads over all the clouds.
And breaks through every gap in them.
To spread hides for drying.
Do not knead on Pesach.
The heat of the sun on a cloudy day.
A sealed flask - its mouth is well sealed so the smell does not escape.
When you poke a small hole the smell escapes strongly.
Similarly on a cloudy day the sun is covered and when it breaks through a gap.
It bursts through with great force.
The sun's glare on a cloudy day - blindness (Gen. 19) means sun-blindness.
The sun piercing through small cloud gaps is harder on the eyes.
The sun that beats down through cloud gaps.
Than the sun visible in open sky.
The sun's heat on a cloudy day is like a sealed flask.
And the sign is a leaky roof - drip water is harder than full immersion.
A flask of vinegar - whose mouth is well-sealed.
When you poke a small opening the smell emerges strongly.
So too on a cloudy day.
The sun, covered and hidden, bursts through cloud gaps with force.
The sign is: sun-blindness from the sun on a cloudy day.
Glare - blindness (sanverim) as translated in Aramaic: brilliance of the sun.
Sanverim in Gen. 19 is translated as 'sun-brilliance.'
The sun piercing through small gaps with force.
Whoever looks toward the gap is stricken with eye-blindness.
More than one who stares at the open sun.
The sign: a dripping roof - drip-water falling on a person is harder.
Than immersing the whole body in water.
The sign: dripping beats immersion.
Sinful thoughts.

display.whats: A dispute about the exact Hebrew formula priests used to announce that dawn had broken - 'Is the entire sky illuminated?' vs 'Has the morning star risen?'
display.hint: The formula mattered practically: the wrong dawn call could trigger a premature tamid slaughter.

learning.summary: A baraita records a dispute about the precise words used in the Temple's dawn announcement. One version: 'Has the morning star [Hagever] appeared over Hebron?' Another: 'Is the entire eastern sky illuminated as far as Hebron?' The Gemara analyzes the implications of each formula for the timing of the service.
learning.ahaMoment: The exact words of the dawn formula weren't just ceremonial - they defined the precise moment when the tamid service was permitted to begin.
learning.memoryAnchor: Two dawn formulas: one asks about the morning star, one about the lit sky. Both point to the same threshold: confirmed dawn.
learning.openQuestion: Why would the Temple need two different formulas for dawn? Was one used only on specific days?

argumentFlow.label: Dawn formula dispute
argumentFlow.text: A baraita records a dispute about whether the dawn announcement asked about the morning star or the illumination of the eastern sky.

argumentFlow.label: Inference from the mishna's formula
argumentFlow.text: The Gemara analyzes which formula is correct based on the subsequent mishna's language about the eastern sky.

narrative: baraita dispute about exact Temple formula

misconceptions: The dawn announcement was purely ceremonial - it had direct halachic significance for when the tamid service could begin.

visualizableElements.label: Two dawn formulas
visualizableElements.description: Formula A: morning star over Hebron. Formula B: entire eastern sky illuminated.

quizSeeds.q: What were the two competing formulas for the dawn announcement in the Temple?
quizSeeds.a: One asked whether the morning star (Hagever) had appeared over Hebron; the other asked whether the entire eastern sky was illuminated.

sugyot.finalRuling: The exact formula remains a dispute; the practical threshold is confirmed dawn before the tamid may be slaughtered.

display.whats: Rav Safra says the afternoon prayer time derives from Abraham observing when walls begin to darken. Rav Yosef challenges: can we derive law from pre-Sinai Patriarchal practice? Rava clarifies. The Gemara then affirms that Abraham observed the entire Torah including eruv tavshilin.
display.hint: Abraham's observance of all Torah commandments before they were given is derived from 'because Abraham obeyed My voice and kept My charge, commandments, statutes and laws' (Genesis 26:5).

learning.summary: Rav Safra teaches that the mincha (afternoon prayer) time begins when walls start to blacken with midday shade. He derives this from Abraham who prayed mincha in this manner. Rav Yosef objects: Abraham lived before the Torah was given - can his practice establish law? Rava clarifies that Rav Yosef's objection was not that Abraham failed to observe these things, but that pre-Sinai practice isn't a valid halachic source. The Gemara then cites Rav: Abraham observed the entire Torah, including the rabbinic eruv tavshilin, derived from the multiple terms in 'kept My charge, commandments, statutes and laws' (Genesis 26:5).
learning.ahaMoment: Abraham's observance of the entire Torah - before it was given - is not presented as miraculous foreknowledge but as spiritual-moral alignment: someone of Abraham's level of divine awareness naturally observed divine will even before its formal revelation.
learning.memoryAnchor: Abraham = the Torah's first self-taught practitioner. Every 'statute' and 'law' in Genesis 26:5 corresponds to a category of Torah observance he kept voluntarily.
learning.openQuestion: If Abraham observed the entire Torah voluntarily, why wasn't the Torah simply given to him? What was added at Sinai beyond the content that Abraham already practiced?

argumentFlow.label: Rav Safra: mincha time from Abraham
argumentFlow.text: Rav Safra: the afternoon prayer time begins when walls begin to blacken with shade - derived from Abraham's practice.

argumentFlow.label: Rav Yosef: pre-Sinai practice is not a legal source
argumentFlow.text: Rav Yosef: Abraham lived before the Torah was given - his practice cannot establish halachic rulings.

argumentFlow.label: Rava: Yosef's point is methodological, not factual
argumentFlow.text: Rava clarifies: Rav Yosef didn't deny that Abraham prayed mincha, only that deriving halacha from his pre-Sinai conduct is invalid methodology.

argumentFlow.label: Egypt had Torah academies
argumentFlow.text: Even in Egypt, Jewish elders maintained Torah learning - supported by 'Go and gather the elders of Israel' (Exodus 3).

argumentFlow.label: Rav: Abraham kept entire Torah
argumentFlow.text: Rav: Abraham observed the entire Torah, as derived from Genesis 26:5 - 'because Abraham obeyed My voice, kept My charge, commandments, statutes and laws.'

argumentFlow.label: Abraham even kept eruv tavshilin
argumentFlow.text: The multiple terms ('charge, commandments, statutes, laws') in the verse teach that Abraham observed even rabbinic enactments like eruv tavshilin.

halachic: pre-Sinai Patriarchal practice as halachic source
narrative: Rav Yosef vs Rava on Patriarchal derivations

misconceptions: Abraham's Torah observance is merely legendary - the Gemara derives it from the specific Hebrew vocabulary in Genesis 26:5.
The Torah was entirely unknown before Sinai - Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob voluntarily observed it through spiritual intuition and divine guidance.

visualizableElements.label: Abraham's Torah observance timeline
visualizableElements.description: Pre-Sinai: Abraham voluntarily keeps all Torah categories

visualizableElements.label: Genesis 26:5 proof-text
visualizableElements.description: 'Kept My charge (mishmarti), commandments (mitzvotai), statutes (chukotai), and laws (torotai)' - each term mapping to a Torah category

quizSeeds.q: What biblical verse does Rav use to prove that Abraham observed the entire Torah?
quizSeeds.a: Genesis 26:5: 'because Abraham obeyed My voice and kept My charge, My commandments, My statutes, and My laws' - the multiple terms correspond to different categories of Torah observance.

quizSeeds.q: What was Rav Yosef's objection to deriving the mincha time from Abraham?
quizSeeds.a: Abraham lived before the Torah was given at Sinai - his personal practice cannot serve as a halachic source, even if he did observe these things.

sugyot.finalRuling: Abraham observed the entire Torah voluntarily. His practice cannot be used as a direct halachic source (post-Sinai standards apply), but it demonstrates the Torah's eternal character.

display.whats: Matya ben Shmuel's mishna question: 'Is the entire eastern sky illuminated as far as Hebron?' The Gemara asks who posed this question - someone on the ground or someone elevated.
display.hint: The questioner's position mattered: someone on the ground couldn't see as far as someone elevated, affecting the reliability of the dawn announcement.

learning.summary: The mishna records that Matya ben Shmuel would announce the dawn by asking 'Is the entire eastern sky illuminated as far as Hebron?' The Gemara raises two possibilities: either someone on the ground asked (unable to see as far) and someone elevated answered, or vice versa. The position of the person announcing dawn affected the reliability of the announcement.
learning.ahaMoment: The Gemara's concern about who stands where is not trivial - the person physically positioned higher could see the horizon earlier, affecting when the tamid service could legitimately begin.
learning.memoryAnchor: Dawn announcer: higher position = earlier dawn visibility = more reliable timing.
learning.openQuestion: Why did the Temple need a specific person (Matya ben Shmuel) to announce dawn rather than having any priest do so?

argumentFlow.label: Mishna: Matya ben Shmuel's dawn question
argumentFlow.text: The mishna records Matya ben Shmuel asking 'Is the entire eastern sky illuminated as far as Hebron?' as the official dawn announcement.

argumentFlow.label: Who asks - ground or elevated?
argumentFlow.text: The Gemara asks: was the questioner standing on the ground (looking up) or elevated (seeing the horizon better)?

argumentFlow.label: Two positions: question and answer
argumentFlow.text: Perhaps: the person on the ground asks; the person elevated confirms by seeing the horizon more clearly.

narrative: question of position for dawn announcement

misconceptions: The dawn announcement was informal - it was a formal declaration by a designated Temple official.

visualizableElements.label: Temple elevated position
visualizableElements.description: The lookout point from which the eastern horizon toward Hebron was visible

visualizableElements.label: Dawn announcement procedure
visualizableElements.description: Official asks - elevated observer confirms - service begins

quizSeeds.q: What specific question did Matya ben Shmuel ask as the official dawn announcement?
quizSeeds.a: 'Is the entire eastern sky illuminated as far as Hebron?' - the standard formula to confirm dawn had broken.

quizSeeds.q: Why does the Gemara care whether the dawn announcer stood on the ground or elevated?
quizSeeds.a: Someone elevated could see the horizon more clearly and confirm dawn earlier - the position affected the reliability and timing of the announcement.

sugyot.finalRuling: The dawn announcement was made from a position that could observe the eastern horizon toward Hebron.

display.whats: The mishna explains why the dawn check was necessary: once, priests slaughtered the tamid before dawn when moonlight over Hebron was mistaken for daylight. Rav Nachman adds that cloudy-day sun is more harmful than direct sunlight.
display.hint: This confusion incident explains why the formalized dawn announcement procedure was instituted - without it, moonlight could be mistaken for dawn.

learning.summary: The mishna reveals the incident that necessitated the formalized dawn check: once during the year (not Yom Kippur), the bright moonlight over Hebron was mistaken for dawn, and priests prematurely slaughtered the daily offering. When the mistake was discovered they had to send the animal to pasture until a new offering could be brought. The Gemara then cites Rav Nachman on the harms of cloudy-day sunlight, and the distinction between complete darkness and the partial light of a cloudy day.
learning.ahaMoment: The dawn announcement formula wasn't invented for formality - it was created in direct response to a real error that caused a tamid to be slaughtered at the wrong time.
learning.memoryAnchor: The moonlight over Hebron looked like dawn. One mistake - one premature slaughter - established the permanent dawn-check protocol.
learning.openQuestion: When the premature tamid was discovered to be invalid, what happened to it? Was a new tamid brought at the correct time?

argumentFlow.label: The confusion incident
argumentFlow.text: The mishna explains: once, priests mistook the bright moonlight over Hebron for dawn and prematurely slaughtered the tamid. The animal had to be sent to pasture.

argumentFlow.label: Practical significance of cloudy day
argumentFlow.text: The Gemara asks: what is the practical difference between a cloudy day and complete darkness?

argumentFlow.label: Rav Nachman: cloudy sun is more harmful
argumentFlow.text: Rav Nachman: the diffuse sunlight on a cloudy day is actually more harmful (to eyesight) than direct sunlight.

misconceptions: The dawn announcement was a purely precautionary formality - it was established in direct response to an actual error of premature tamid slaughter.

visualizableElements.label: The confusion incident
visualizableElements.description: Bright moonlight over Hebron mistaken for dawn - premature tamid slaughter - animal sent to pasture

visualizableElements.label: Moonlight vs dawn light vs cloudy-day light
visualizableElements.description: Three types of morning light and their effects on dawn perception

quizSeeds.q: What incident led to the formalization of the dawn announcement in the Temple?
quizSeeds.a: Once, priests mistook the bright moonlight over Hebron for dawn and prematurely slaughtered the daily offering - the formalized dawn-check procedure was instituted to prevent recurrence.

quizSeeds.q: What does Rav Nachman say about the light on a cloudy day compared to direct sunlight?
quizSeeds.a: Rav Nachman says the diffuse light on a cloudy day is actually more harmful than direct sunlight.

sugyot.finalRuling: The dawn announcement formula was instituted after a real confusion incident. A cloudy day resembles a fully dark night for timing purposes.
